intro: Gilead (meaning the heap or mass of testimony) was the mountainous region east of the Jordan River, extending from the Yarmuk River in the north to the Arnon in the south, roughly corresponding to modern northern Jordan. It was a fertile land of forests, pasture, and spice-producing trees. The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh settled there after the conquest (Num. 32:1–5). Gilead gave its name to the famous "balm of Gilead," a healing resin widely traded in the ancient world (Gen. 37:25; Jer. 8:22). The region features prominently in the narratives of Jacob and Laban (Gen. 31), the judge Jephthah, the prophet Elijah, and later the Transjordanian campaigns of the divided monarchy. Gilead was among the first territories conquered by the Assyrians under Tiglath-pileser III (2 Kings 15:29).
hitchcock_meaning: the heap or mass of testimony
